posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
The mobile UX is terrible. The biggest call to action is the “check price” button, it’s very difficult to actually get into any reviews without accidentally clicking on that. They’ve also 3x’d the amount of vertical real estate for each item, so if you’re looking at a long list like “bordeaux 2022”, you have to scroll 3x as far to see the same amount of information.

The bigger problem is that they’ve changed their search and display hierarchy. You used to be able to search for “Domaine Joblot” and easily see all the cuvees in one place, and then click down on each to see all vintages. That’s impossible now. Likewise, if you’re trying to find Chateau Latour, you cannot just see Latour, you have to also see the 2nd and 3rd wines. There’s no way to filter them out. Super irritating.

posts.name: Brian Befano
posts.cooked:
Two quick points

  1. The ‘Vintage chart’ is no longer a chart, makes sense to rename the heading to ‘Vintage Guide’ to accurately reflect the content. I would also bring back the actual chart, the information density in the old chart is much higher, it’s a better quick summary. I like the idea of a vintage guide, but include more detailed information which will differentiate it from the chart.

  2. The ability to search the prior notes, filter down to results, and then expand all notes and browse was very nice. Now, to read two different tasting notes I have one click to get in, two to get back, three to get back in, and four to get back out. The additional work increases linearly 2x for every note I want to read, so not a big deal if I only want to read a single note, but makes browsing basically impossible.

Last little point, and I now you did not explicitly ask for feedback on this forum and this thread is just us spontaneously complaining, but I get annoyed when I pay for a product and then also have to beta test and make suggestions. Maybe offer a free month during roll-out and solicit feedback.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
Love the spelling/diacritical tolerance feature!

One example of something I think could/should be better - I did a search for a wine I thought would have multiple reviews - 1986 Mouton. Auto-complete suggested different wines it thought I meant (another good new feature) and I chose the Chateau Mouton-Rothschild.

Doing that on another well-known site which may previously have been mentioned in this thread pulls all of their reviews up on a single page, with the text of each note in one column, and also (among others) the score, suggested drinking window, author, and review date. I can sort by each of these, which is also great (I like to sort by review date so the most recent note is shown first, when all the reviews are for a single vintage of a single wine, as in this example).

On TWA, it pulls up one review. The screen shows me, in the “header” above the note, in easy-to-spot fashion, the score, the reviewer’s name, the release price, and the suggested drinking window. Then at the bottom it shows 9 boxes which tell me that there are 9 total reviews and I have to click on number 2 or number 7 or number 9 if I want to go read additional reviews beyond the one on the screen. Why is the one on the screen the one it defaults to? I assume it’s the most recently published note, but nothing tells me that.

Problems IMO - 1. The “release price” is shown as $899.98. I wasn’t shopping for first growths at the time, but I’m pretty sure the 1986 was released for less than $900 per bottle.

  1. The date of the review should be part of the easy-to-spot header, along with the name of the reviewer. Some of the reviews note this information near the end of the text, some don’t, but I don’t like having to hunt for it.

  2. Not putting all the reviews on one page means I need to click again and again to see all the reviews, and also the way it is set up I have no way of knowing what those other reviews are. For example, the review that pops up is by Neal Martin from 2016. If I want to see more reviews by NM, or if I don’t care what he thinks and only want to see reviews by Robert Parker, I can’t sort by reviewer name like I can on that other site, nor can I easily determine from that first page whether review 2 or 3 or 4 (on up to 9) are by NM or RMPJr or LPB or WK or whomever. Likewise if I’m only interested in reviews published since, say 2010. I have to click on each of the nine “pages” to find out who wrote each review and (sometimes) when it was published. The “all one one page” table format is much more useful.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
Just played around a little more. If I type 1986 mouton rothschild and hit enter, instead of starting to type and then clicking on the name of the wine when it’s suggested, I get a different view, part of which is this:

image892×332 14.4 KB

Issues with this -

The database contains reviews by NM, RMPJr, and LPB. Yet the “author” filter only lists the first two. Also, the (1) next to each author’s name seems to indicate that there is 1 review by each in the database, but NM and RMPJr each have more than 1 review among the 9 total in the database.

The results in the main page (to the right of the filter boxes) list the wine and the score, but not the text of the TN, or the date of the review, or the suggested drinking window, etc. To get any of that, I have to click on the name of the wine and then it takes me to the page I discussed in my prior post.

The filter boxes don’t seem to do anything. For example, when I check the box for RMPJr as the reviewer, I get a brief page re-load and then everything still looks the same. Then when I click on the name of the wine it still takes me to that same page discussed above, showing the NM review as the first of 9 instead of only taking me to the RMPJr reviews.

Separately, it seems that auto-complete only works for wine names? For example, if I want to search for “1995 pomerol,” auto-complete suggests different 1995 Pomerol wines, but doesn’t just suggest “1995 pomerol” as what I might mean. It would be great if other common searches such as names of appellations, grape varieties, etc. could be worked into the auto-complete database.

Just editing to add more as I find them - I just clicked on EB’s new Sonoma article from the main page. From the article, I clicked on “View All Wines.” It then took me to a list of all the wines, but the default sort is “latest reviews” - a default I love when as above I’m doing a search for something like 1986 Mouton. But when I’m using the button to show all the wines from a new article, obviously all the wines will have the same review date so it’s a bit of a useless default.

As they display, they seem to be sorted by score, except that three Turley wines with low-90s scores are shown first, before a 99-point DuMol wine is listed fourth, with descending scores after that.

In the sort options, alpha by producer is not an option, only score, vintage, or review date. When reading all the wines by article, obviously revew date is useless as a sort and vintage is mostly useless since so many of the wines will be the same vintage or two. Score is useful, but alphabetical by producer would also be useful (and my preferred sort when viewing all the wines reviewed in a new article).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The vintage chart used to be more detailed, but is it actually meaningful? Honestly, anyone who thinks you can generalize about vintages as a whole like that is so lost that I’m not sure there’s any way to help them. Roumier will still taste too young when Drouhin is fully mature; the rainfall that helped one vineyard in Beaune in a given vintage might not have fallen on the neighbor (I see this first hand); one producer didn’t beat mildew in 2018, another did; etc etc. In my view, a vintage chart can only give very broad brush strokes, and anything beyond that is peddling an illusion.

I think you’d instantly become the Greatest Hero In all of wine criticism if you made it your mission to abolish vintage charts.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
William, you are absolutely right about this. But (totally unasked for business advice) you should do it anyway, and with as much detail as you can stand. Hell, give a different rating for every vintage for every commune/village! The people who hate vintage charts will ignore it. But the people who want to use them, or need them, or think they need them, will love it. And being the MOST detailed vintage chart will give you cred/score points above other sources. You can even publish it with the proviso that it gives a rough sense of the vintage, and should be used in conjunction with written comments.

Of course, if this is just too cynical, then ignore this feedback .

posts.name: Max Swomley
posts.cooked:
Sure, here are some notes from 5 minutes of browsing:

Things I don’t like:
-Can’t view a single vintage with different regions in the vintage chart. Even if the chart is only good for generalities, I have to assume this is a decent-sized draw for the site based on how often I search for a vintage and end up on rp.com from it
-The link for “The Wine Advocate” in the top nav bar is dead, it could easily link to the latest issues (same with the other dropdowns)

Things I do like
-Very clean and easy to read
-Editorial Calendar is great, easy to find. Not sure I ever saw it on the old site
-I figured out how the search works in regards to articles, that was majorly bugging me and a bit check off the list

Things
-For the top search bar, it would be nice to have the option to search by article or tasting note. That said, I mostly browse from my browser and not mobile and it’s not that big of a deal

posts.name: Vince T s 3 n g
posts.cooked:
You usually have such a fine ear for your customer, but in this case I think you’re wrong. The mass RP subscriber is not a berserker drinking Roumier. They’re still trying to grok the differences between Chambolle and Gevrey (or maybe even Bordeaux and Burgundy), and in the cases of vintages, the differences between years like 2021 and 2022 in Bordeaux. Sure, vintage charts are a caricature, but they’re a useful stepping stone for the beginner exploring a region for the first time. They could absolutely use a rethink of what info is conveyed and provide appropriate caveats and enticements to learn more, but they shouldn’t be abandoned wholesale.

To use a music metaphor, a sizable portion of your audience is still trying to appreciate the difference between Beethoven and Tchaikovsky, and you’ve made the argument that anyone who doesn’t already recognize the signatures of middle vs late Beethoven is a hopeless case and should not be educated. I encourage you to engage those audiences as well as berserkers… I think you are one of the wine industry’s best hopes at bringing a new generation of wine enthusiasts into the fold and you need to be mindful of what each segment of your audience needs to get there.

Searching on Cellar Tracker and/or a general internet search usually provides more useful information.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: That might mean they haven’t been loaded yet. That has happened to me in the past.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked:
Surely, but it seems the article should perhaps not be posted until the wines are loaded?

In any event, they are loaded now.

Playing around a bit, I see that I can use the check box filter by producer name to look at all wines from any given producer, and it works very fast so that’s not a bad substitute for the ability to sort alpha by producer, though I’d still like that to be added to the sort options.

Another observation is that the price is not shown in the “all wines” view. If I take a long list of unfamiliar wines from a new article and sort by rating, I am very interested in seeing the price information (when it’s available). An unfamiliar (to me) $18 bottle with a 94 point rating and an unfamiliar (to me) $250 bottle with a 94 point rating mean very different things to me. In this case, these four wines range from $45 to $200 (release price) but I have to click on each wine name one at a time to see that. It would be nice to have the release price somewhere in this initial view:

image801×370 14.8 KB

posts.name: David Cooper
posts.cooked: I access it through the tasting notes on CT. An icon appears bolded beneath the Pro Reviews.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: That’s not the integration he’s talking about. What everyone has been waiting for is the integration with the reviews directly in the Pro Reviews.

posts.name: Josh Felix
posts.cooked: This may mean their site is not caching the results (in state) and code on the page is not going retrieve the result set again.

posts.name: Ⓑⓞⓑⓑⓨ Beachy
posts.cooked:
Perhaps not related to the UX, but the “find it online” experience is reliably unreliable, at least for a few wines I’ve looked at today. Zero of my clicks resulted in the correct wine at a retailer.

As an example, I was looking up the Fevre 1er Montmains 2020, and all of the clicks to external sites resulted in a wine from a different wine-maker. It’s a great way to learn about other “Montmains,” though!

robertparker.com

Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

Web site created using create-react-app

Same problem with…

robertparker.com

Robert Parker Wine Advocate - Unbiased Wine Reviews

Web site created using create-react-app

A few Bordeaux wines seemed to link fine. To be honest, I rarely use the feature because Wine-Searcher is so excellent, especially with the CT integration, but I thought someone might want to know.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked: I found a typo that the typo recognizer didn’t recognize - Lannesan vs Lanessan. FWIW.

posts.name: J Fernandez
posts.cooked: just changed a bit the algorithm to handle that use case, thanks for sharing! Sometimes it has undesired side effects on other searches as too much tolerance in typo can bring lots of homonyms. We’ll keep finetuning

posts.name: J Fernandez
posts.cooked: This is a wine searcher API call where we pass through lot of wine information to retrieve retailers form them. We really need to have perfectly accurate results and more user centered. We are starting the conversation with them to ensure perfect match. Thank you for sharing those use cases.

posts.name: J Fernandez
posts.cooked: That’s a latency between article publication and tasting notes indexation. We will be rid of that very soon.

posts.name: Dav3_Dyr0ff
posts.cooked: Sounds great. Given the lack of latency on the reader end between looking at the article and going to the notes, having the notes fully indexed before the articles go live seems like the right play to me.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
JacoboF:

just changed a bit the algorithm to handle that use case, thanks for sharing! Sometimes it has undesired side effects on other searches as too much tolerance in typo can bring lots of homonyms. We’ll keep finetuning

thanks for sharing updates here. what are you using for search?

posts.name: J Fernandez
posts.cooked: We’re using Algolia.Not the neural one, but the basic tool.

posts.cooked: The new UI looks good. One thing I would like to be able to do is see all producers covered in a given article - I could do this on the previous site, but on the new version it only shows the top few

posts.name: Luis Gutierrez
posts.cooked:
Wow! It took me a long time to recover my user profile, but I’m back!
Yes, I agree, not seeing prices annoys me too. I’ve already mentioned to Jacobo and it’s good that you also mention it.

Thanks!

posts.name: Max Swomley
posts.cooked: Definitely would love to see prices!

posts.name: Matthew Lee
posts.cooked: One thing about the old UX I miss was being able to read the body text of a review by “mousing over” the name instead of clicking on the wine itself. It saved a lot of time in larger tasting articles.

posts.name: Max Swomley
posts.cooked: A preview without clicking in is a big selling point these days. Vinous does a good job with their dropdown, though it feels a little clunky.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: @JacoboF I’d imagine you already know, but the biggest issue is that you do a bunch of filtering on the search page, click into a wine and click back and the search is gone. Ideally the search would update the URL so that you could copy and paste a search to someone else, but at the very least it needs to cache and reload the latest search.

posts.name: J Fernandez
posts.cooked: Absolutely, thanks

posts.name: J Fernandez
posts.cooked: It should cork better now, thanks again

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
If Wine Advocate scores are integrated into CellarTracker that will justify my annual subscription for sure. I prefer your reviews William to the gnomic and inscrutable prose of BH. But its so cumbersome to log into WA and do a separate wine search that I never do it.

Loved your 21’ 20’ Burgundy vintage review by the way.

posts.name: Steve Mclaughlin
posts.cooked:
I’m with @Andrew_K on this. I run a website-based business and I think 1850s Prussian general and early internet-adopter Helmuth Von Moltke had it right:
“No new website design survives contact with the customer”

Iteration is the way to a robust site.

No matter how hard we work to interview, listen and develop new features, once you let something out into the wild interwebs, there will be bugs to fix, moles to whack and squeaky wheels to oil.

Some of the feedback will be run-of-the-mill grousing (though that would NEVER happen here…)
But a lot is important guidance on what’s broke and what our users really want. The squeaky wheels are painful to hear, but we ignore them at our peril.

Credit to @William_Kelley and @JacoboF for listening to the specific feedback WBers have posted here. I’d give the developers a couple months.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked:
JacoboF:

It should cork better now, thanks again

bravo!

posts.name: J Fernandez
posts.cooked: You will be the first to know

posts.name: Eric LeVine
posts.cooked: I hope I would know first…

posts.name: Brad England
posts.cooked: This isn’t the first time this has been said, but I find it ironic that if they allowed integration it’s likely better for WA economically. Right now, I cut and paste William’s reviews for wines I buy. And if he left, I’d probably stop subscribing and still have his reviews in CT. If they integrate, I won’t bother, and if he left I might keep my subscription for a while. Can anyone think of a rational reason they don’t integrate? Back in the day I assumed it was just RP’s stubbornness. But what rational reason can they have today?

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
Brad_England:

Can anyone think of a rational reason they don’t integrate

I think William said they are working on it now. I asked Eric about it at a dinner recently and he said TWA had a pay per hit model to their data and that they didn’t want to just offer it for free as an add on to paid subscribers. So they were at an impasse for a few years. But maybe they have a path forward now.

posts.name: Brad England
posts.cooked: I saw William said that, but will believe it only when it happens. At least that explanation makes some sense economically. But long term, businesses that disregard their core customer’s desires usually pay the price for that. I assume subscribers are core for WA, but maybe that’s not true. They’ve been somewhat customer unfriendly for a long time, and many of us came back for more.

posts.name: Mark Morr i ssette
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

he said TWA had a pay per hit model

That is interesting and a possible explanation… I fully agree with @Brad_England.

As it stands today, I will likely be a Vinous subscriber for a very, very, very long time. I want their reviews to stay in CT. Period.

I am really enjoying TWA, but if I ever decide I want to cut back my spend on reviewing sites, it will be easy to cut: I have already cut and pasted everything I want into CT. That changes the minute the integrate with CT.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
That is very much true!

But, it has been somewhat difficult to explain what CT integration actually means to people who don’t use CT. But, keep the faith! I lobbied for this hard at Decanter, who implemented it shortly after I left, so I have a track record of getting this done.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
You can cut and paste the Vinous reviews and not run into that problem. I do that for precisely that reason (and because their integration isn’t perfect and often has considerable lags).

Given that it isn’t that hard to cut and paste into your own personal reviews, I do wonder if by “integration” people mean the ability to search the reviews whether or not they own the wine, something, for instance, that you can do with Mosel Fine Wines (which is admittedly a free subscription). I’ve seen people mention that if TWA was integrated into CT, they would never go to the TWA website again, which I found odd; how would you be able to read the articles?

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked:
crickey:

I’ve seen people mention that if TWA was integrated into CT, they would never go to the TWA website again, which I found odd; how would you be able to read the articles?

I’ve never read an article. I’m guessing others mainly use the subscription for the individual reviews and scores.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
1. I do read the articles.
  2. I meant “read the articles” in a more extended sense of sitting down to read the reviews in an article from front to back. It may be a legacy of my print subscription, but it is the way I prefer to read it. I also prefer to read the article in producer order, so one of my complaints, which I voiced earlier in the thread, is that I can’t sort by producer anymore. I feel like I’m reading a jumbled mess currently.

posts.name: Max Swomley
posts.cooked: To be fair, thats a lot of cutting and pasting especially if you buy en prim BDX. Also, doesnt vinous disallow copying on its website?

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: It can be, although I find it satisfying to enter. In a total dick move, Vinous did disable copying, although there are imperfect workarounds.

posts.name: Max Swomley
posts.cooked: Yea, ive found those effective enough, but they still suck

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: I definitely read the articles. They often provide important context.

I tell my wife I only subscribe for the articles.

But wow… having the reviews automatically post in in CT would be better than sex.
